Affiliation (institution(s)) (string or N/A)
Bullet / PyBullet: Bullet Physics open-source community (originally by Erwin Coumans)
CoppeliaSim (V-REP): Coppelia Robotics AG (Zurich, Switzerland); originally developed within Toshiba R&D
MuJoCo: Google DeepMind (acquired October 2021); originally developed at University of Washington Movement Control Lab by Emanuel Todorov; commercialized by Roboti LLC
OpenRAVE: Carnegie Mellon University Robotics Institute / Quality of Life Technology Center (originated 2006); later maintained at JSK Robotics Lab, University of Tokyo; primary maintainer: Rosen Diankov (MUJIN Inc.)
PhysX (open-source SDK): NVIDIA Corporation; originally developed by NovodeX AG (Switzerland); acquired by Ageia Technologies in 2004; acquired by NVIDIA in 2008
AirSim: Microsoft Research (developed at Microsoft AI & Research); original team led by Shital Shah, Debadeepta Dey, and Ashish Kapoor
Drake: MIT Computer Science and Artificial Intelligence Laboratory (CSAIL), Robot Locomotion Group (originated); primary development now by Toyota Research Institute (TRI); additional contributions from MIT, CMU, and the broader robotics community
DART: Georgia Institute of Technology, Graphics Lab and Humanoid Robotics Lab (originated 2011, created by C. Karen Liu and Mike Stilman); University of Washington Personal Robotics Lab; Open Source Robotics Foundation; Stanford University Movement Lab; Carnegie Mellon University
CARLA: Computer Vision Center (CVC), Universitat Autònoma de Barcelona / Universitat Politècnica de Catalunya (UPC), Barcelona; Intel Labs; Embodied AI Foundation; collaborative open-source community
MORSE: LAAS-CNRS (Laboratoire d'Analyse et d'Architecture des Systèmes, French National Center for Scientific Research), Toulouse, France; jointly developed with ONERA (French aerospace lab); contributions from 15+ universities worldwide
Simbody: Simbios, NIH National Center for Physics-Based Simulation of Biological Structures at Stanford University; primary developers Michael Sherman and Peter Eastman (Stanford Simbios Center); Scott Delp Neuromuscular Biomechanics Lab (Stanford)
OMPL (Open Motion Planning Library): Kavraki Lab, Rice University (lead development); Dr. Lydia Kavraki (PI), Dr. Mark Moll, Dr. Zachary Kingston, Dr. Ioan Șucan
Pinocchio: Inria (French National Institute for Research in Digital Science and Technology), Paris; LAAS-CNRS (Laboratoire d'Analyse et d'Architecture des Systèmes, Toulouse); primary developer: Justin Carpentier (Inria Willow team); co-creator: Nicolas Mansard (LAAS-CNRS Gepetto team)
OROCOS: KU Leuven / PMA Division, Belgium; founded by Herman Bruyninckx (KU Leuven, December 2000); initial implementation by Peter Soetens (PhD student KU Leuven); copyright shared by 20+ contributors internationally
SOFA: Inria (France); four active Inria teams: Mimesis, Imagine, Defrost, Asclepios; founded 2006 by Inria alongside USTL, UJF, CNRS, and MGH (Massachusetts General Hospital)
MoveIt!: PickNik Robotics (PickNik Inc.) leads development; originally developed by Sachin Chitta and Dave Coleman at Willow Garage / SRI International; maintained by broad ROS community
GraspIt!: Columbia University Robotics Group (Professor Peter Allen, lab of origin); current maintainers: Matei Ciocarlie (Columbia Robotics & Rehabilitation Lab), Jake Varley (Google), Jon Weisz; historical contributors at Columbia and Carnegie Mellon
Habitat: Meta AI Research (FAIR — Fundamental AI Research); developed by Facebook AI Research; AI Habitat project part of FAIR's embodied AI research program led by researchers including Erik Wijmans, Abhishek Kadian, Amir Sadeghi, Claudia Perez-D'Arpino, and many others
Project Chrono: University of Wisconsin-Madison (primary lead; Dan Negrut, Simulation-Based Engineering Lab) and University of Parma, Italy (co-developer; Alessandro Tasora); supported by NSF, US Army, and other funders
Flightmare: Robotics and Perception Group (RPG), University of Zurich and ETH Zurich; lead developers: Yunlong Song (ETH Zurich RPG), Selim Nusbaum, and team; PI: Davide Scaramuzza (ETH Zurich RPG director)
MRPT: University of Almería (Spain); lead developer: Jose Luis Blanco-Claraco (Full Professor at University of Almería, formerly TU Munich and University of Málaga); founded 2005 by Jose Luis Blanco
Newton Dynamics: Independent open-source project; primary developer: Julio Jerez; co-developer: Alain Suero (original co-author); community contributions via GitHub
YARP: Istituto Italiano di Tecnologia (IIT), Genoa, Italy; RobotCub Consortium (EU-funded iCub humanoid robot project); primary developers: Lorenzo Natale, Daniele Pagnin, and IIT Robotics team; originates from humanoid robot research (iCub)
OpenRTM-aist: National Institute of Advanced Industrial Science and Technology (AIST), Japan; primary developer: Noriaki Ando (AIST Intelligent Systems Research Institute); OpenRTM-aist Project team at AIST

Metric: Software purpose (string)
Gazebo: Open-source robotics simulator providing high-fidelity physics, rendering, and sensor models for robot simulation and testing
Webots: Open-source multi-platform robot simulator for modeling, programming, and simulating robots, vehicles, and mechanical systems
Bullet / PyBullet: Python module for physics simulation, robotics, and deep reinforcement learning based on Bullet Physics SDK
CoppeliaSim (V-REP): Versatile and scalable robot simulation framework for rapid algorithm development, factory automation simulation, fast prototyping, verification, and digital twin creation
MuJoCo: General-purpose physics simulator for research and development in robotics, biomechanics, graphics and animation, and machine learning requiring fast and accurate simulation of articulated structures
ODE (Open Dynamics Engine): Open-source, high-performance library for simulating rigid body dynamics; used in robotics, games, and interactive simulation applications
ROS 2 (Robot Operating System 2): Open-source software development kit (SDK) and middleware framework for robotics: provides communication infrastructure (DDS-based pub/sub), hardware abstraction, drivers, libraries, visualizers, tools, and community packages for building robot applications
OpenRAVE: Open-source robotics planning framework for testing, developing, and deploying motion planning algorithms; focuses on simulation and analysis of kinematic and geometric information for robot motion planning, especially manipulation
PhysX (open-source SDK): High-performance, GPU-accelerated physics engine for simulating rigid body dynamics, soft body dynamics, cloth, fluid, and particle systems; primarily used in video games, robotics simulation, and autonomous vehicle development
AirSim: Open-source simulator for autonomous vehicles (drones/quadrotors and ground vehicles) built on Unreal Engine; supports testing AI systems, computer vision, deep learning, and reinforcement learning for autonomous vehicle research; provides photorealistic environments, physics simulation, and sensor models (cameras, LiDAR, IMU)
Drake: Open-source C++ and Python toolbox for model-based design and verification of robotics systems; focuses on multibody dynamics, trajectory optimization, model predictive control, perception, manipulation planning, and formal verification for robustly safe robots
DART: Open-source C++ physics engine and kinematics/dynamics library for robotics and computer animation; provides kinematic and dynamic simulation of articulated structures with an emphasis on accurate and stable simulation for manipulation and locomotion research
CARLA: Open-source autonomous driving simulator built on Unreal Engine; provides photorealistic urban environments, accurate physics, and a comprehensive sensor suite (cameras, LiDAR, radar, IMU, GPS) for developing, training, and validating autonomous driving systems and AI agents
MORSE: Generic academic robotic simulator based on the Blender 3D engine and its integrated Bullet physics engine; provides a component-based architecture for simulating robots and their interactions with environments, emphasizing middleware-agnostic design (ROS, YARP, Pocolibs, etc.)
Simbody: High-performance open-source C++ multibody dynamics library for simulating articulated biomechanical and mechanical systems; provides accurate physics simulation of skeletons, robots, and vehicles; serves as the physics engine underlying OpenSim (biomedical simulation software)
OMPL (Open Motion Planning Library): Open-source C++ library for sampling-based motion planning algorithms; provides implementations of planners such as PRM, RRT, RRT*, EST, and many others; designed as a framework for motion planning research and integration into robotics software (e.g., MoveIt, OpenRAVE)
Pinocchio: Fast and flexible C++ library for rigid-body dynamics computations and their analytical derivatives; implements state-of-the-art algorithms (RNEA, CRBA, ABA, JSIM) for computing kinematics, dynamics, and Jacobians of articulated rigid-body models; includes Python bindings and supports robotics, animation, and optimal control applications
OROCOS: Open-source C++ framework for real-time robot control; major components: Real-Time Toolkit (RTT) for component-based hard real-time software; Kinematics and Dynamics Library (KDL) for serial chain kinematics; Bayesian Filtering Library (BFL); Orocos Component Library (OCL)
SOFA: Open-source real-time medical simulation and interactive simulation framework; provides component-based architecture for deformable object simulation, finite element methods, collision detection, and haptic feedback; primary applications in surgical simulation, medical education, and soft robotics
MoveIt!: Open-source robotics manipulation framework for ROS and ROS 2; provides motion planning, kinematics, collision checking, trajectory execution, and perception integration for robot arms and manipulators; integrates OMPL, CHOMP, STOMP, and other planners as plugins
GraspIt!: Simulation environment for robotic grasping research; supports loading robot hand models and 3D object models to plan, evaluate, and visualize grasps; implements grasp quality metrics (epsilon and volume quality measures), contact model simulation, and the GraspIt! eigengrasp planning framework; integrates Columbia Grasp Database (CGDB)
Habitat: Flexible, high-performance 3D simulator for embodied AI research; enables training and evaluation of embodied AI agents (virtual robots, navigation agents) in photorealistic 3D environments; provides efficient 3D scene rendering (Magnum renderer), dataset support (Matterport3D, Gibson, Replica, HM3D), and physics simulation via Bullet; companion to Habitat-Lab RL training framework
Project Chrono: High-performance open-source multi-physics simulation library; provides multibody dynamics, finite element analysis (FEA), rigid and flexible body simulation, vehicle dynamics (Chrono::Vehicle), granular terrain (Chrono::Granular), fluid-solid interaction (Chrono::FSI), and sensor simulation (Chrono::Sensor); supports GPU-accelerated simulation via CUDA
Flightmare: Flexible modular quadrotor simulator for learning and control research; provides rendering-based environment (Unity3D rendering engine) and physics simulation for training reinforcement learning agents on drone racing and agile flight tasks; modular architecture separates rendering (Unity) from RL environment (Python/C++) for flexibility in fast or photorealistic simulation modes
MRPT: Open-source cross-platform C++ library for mobile robotics research and development; provides algorithms for SLAM (Simultaneous Localization and Mapping), computer vision, motion planning, and sensor data processing; includes a 3D robotics simulation environment (RawLogViewer, SceneViewer3D); supports mapping, localization, path planning, and sensor fusion
Newton Dynamics: High-performance physics simulation engine for games and interactive applications; provides rigid body dynamics, collision detection, soft bodies, vehicle dynamics, and articulated rigid bodies; optimized for real-time deterministic simulation with exact arithmetic; provides Newtonian dynamics solver with an exact implicit solver for rigid body integrations; widely used in game development and robotics simulation
YARP: Open-source middleware for humanoid and general robotics; provides communication infrastructure for distributed robot systems (ports, carriers, protocols); supports inter-process communication between robot sensor and actuator modules; includes libraries for sensor processing, motor control, computer vision, and device abstraction; primary middleware for the iCub humanoid robot platform and used in many academic humanoid robot projects
OpenRTM-aist: Robotic Technology Middleware (RTM) implementation for the OMG-standardized RT (Robot Technology) Component standard; provides a component-based framework for creating, managing and connecting RT-Components (robot software modules); enables distributed robot systems by abstracting hardware and software interfaces into standardized components; includes component framework, execution context management, CORBA-based inter-component communication, and development tools (RTCBuilder, RTSystemEditor); widely used in Japanese robotics industry and research

Metric: Additional comments? (can cover any metrics you feel are missing
Gazebo: or any other thoughts you have), Development began in 2002 at USC. Gazebo Classic (original version) reached EOL January 2025. Current version is Gazebo (formerly Ignition), representing over 16 years of development. Latest release: Jetty (September 2025, LTS version). Maintainer: arjoc@intrinsic.ai
Webots: or any other thoughts you have), Development began in 1996 at EPFL by Dr. Olivier Michel. Commercialized by Cyberbotics Ltd. since 1998. Became free and open-source in December 2018. Over 250,000 downloads since open-sourcing. Beginner-friendly software for robotics education and research.
Bullet / PyBullet: Created by Erwin Coumans, who received a Scientific and Technical Academy Award in 2015 for Bullet Physics. Actively used by Google Robotics for quadruped locomotion, sim-to-real transfer, motion imitation, and reinforcement learning. Can run in web browsers via Google Colab.
CoppeliaSim (V-REP): Formerly known as V-REP, rebranded 2019. CoppeliaSim Edu is free and fully functional for academic use only. CoppeliaSim Pro is commercial. CoppeliaSim Player is a free limited viewer. Coppelia Robotics founded in 2010, acquired by Blue Workforce in 2016.
MuJoCo: Originally developed at University of Washington Movement Control Lab by Emanuel Todorov, Tom Erez, and Yuval Tassa (2012). Commercialized by Roboti LLC. Acquired by Google DeepMind in October 2021; made freely available then open-sourced under Apache-2.0 in May 2022. Industry-standard physics engine for reinforcement learning research.
ODE (Open Dynamics Engine): One of the oldest and most established open-source physics engines, continuously developed since 2001. Widely used as a physics backend in Gazebo (Classic) and CoppeliaSim. The project uses Bitbucket as its primary repository (not GitHub), using Mercurial historically and now Git.
ROS 2 (Robot Operating System 2): ROS 2 is a complete redesign of ROS 1 (Robot Operating System) to address limitations in security, real-time performance, and multi-robot support. Uses DDS (Data Distribution Service) for communication instead of a custom middleware. Has annual releases with LTS versions every two years. Latest LTS: Jazzy Jalisco (May 23, 2024, supported until May 2029).
OpenRAVE: OpenRAVE began as a rewrite of the RAVE simulator (developed by James Kuffner, 1995) by Rosen Diankov at CMU in 2006. It uses a plugin architecture for planners, controllers, and sensors. Stable release is 0.8.2 (2012); current GitHub code is more recent but formal versioning lags. Widely used in manipulation and grasping research. A Python 3 compatible fork (openrave3) exists on GitHub.
PhysX (open-source SDK): PhysX began as NovodeX (2001), was commercialized with dedicated PPU hardware by Ageia, then acquired and GPU-integrated by NVIDIA (2008). Open-sourced in 2018 to support robotics and AI research. PhysX 5 merges formerly separate Flex library capabilities. Used as physics backend in NVIDIA Isaac Sim, Unreal Engine, Unity, and many robotics simulators.
AirSim: AirSim was built on Unreal Engine 4 and also provides a Unity plugin. Microsoft officially archived the repository in November 2022, citing resource constraints. A successor, Microsoft Project AirSim, is under development as a commercial product. Community forks (e.g., Colosseum by CodexLabsLLC) continue active development. v1.8.1 was the final official release.
Drake: Drake (named after Sir Francis Drake) started as a MATLAB toolbox at MIT for dynamic systems analysis. It was rewritten in C++ with Python bindings and is now the primary simulation and planning framework used at Toyota Research Institute. Releases monthly on the last Thursday of each month. Unusually rigorous software engineering practices for a research codebase.
DART: DART began at Georgia Tech in 2011 as a physics engine emphasizing stable simulation for locomotion. Used as a physics backend in Gazebo (alongside ODE and Bullet). Has an active main branch targeting DART 7 with modularization plans, and a release-6.x branch for stable fixes. Python bindings (dartpy) available via PyPI. Published in the Journal of Open Source Software (JOSS) 2018.
CARLA: CARLA (CAR Learning to Act) is one of the most popular autonomous driving simulators globally. Founded in 2016 at the CVC with Intel Labs support; first presented at CoRL 2017. Originally built on Unreal Engine 4; CARLA 0.10.0 (December 2024) migrated to Unreal Engine 5.5. Has an annual CARLA CVPR Autonomous Driving Grand Challenge. Python client API enables scripted scenarios. ScenarioRunner and CARLA-ROS-Bridge are major ecosystem tools.
MORSE: MORSE is built on the Blender 3D engine and uses Blender's Python scripting environment. Unique among simulators for its middleware-agnostic design: supports ROS, YARP, Pocolibs, HLA, and socket-based communication simultaneously. No longer maintained as of ~2020; requires Blender 2.79b (outdated). MORSE was the primary academic robotics simulator in Europe before Gazebo gained dominance. 4,356 total commits over its active life.
Simbody: Simbody originates from NIH/Stanford work on biological simulation. It is the core physics engine for OpenSim, the industry-standard biomechanical simulation software used in clinical gait analysis and prosthetics research. Simbody handles compliant contact, constraint-based simulation, and articulated body inertia efficiently. Has 5,000+ commits. Primary use case is biomedical simulation but also applied to robotics.
OMPL (Open Motion Planning Library): OMPL is the de facto standard C++ motion planning library in academic robotics. It does not include a robot model or collision checking (by design); these must be provided by the user or an integrating framework (MoveIt, ROS, OpenRAVE). OMPL ships with an extensive benchmarking framework for comparing planners. Integrated into MoveIt as default motion planning backend. Latest release: OMPL 1.7.0 (April 7, 2025).
Pinocchio: Pinocchio is among the fastest open-source rigid-body dynamics libraries available. Supports URDF and SRDF model loading. Python bindings (pip install pin) work only on Linux currently. Conda install (conda install pinocchio -c conda-forge) works on all platforms. Widely used in whole-body control, trajectory optimization, and learning-for-control research. Integral to many Inria/LAAS robotics frameworks (HPP, tsid, crocoddyl).
OROCOS: OROCOS is one of the oldest open-source real-time robot control frameworks. KDL became the standard kinematic chain library in ROS 1. ROS robot_state_publisher, trac-ik, and many ROS IK plugins build on KDL. LGPL/GPL runtime exception license was chosen to allow C++ template use without GPL contamination.
SOFA: SOFA is the leading open-source framework for medical simulation. Component-based scene graph architecture allows mixing and matching deformable models, collision algorithms, and solvers. SofaPython3 plugin provides Python scripting API. Used in surgical simulation, catheter navigation planning, and soft robot control. Plugin ecosystem (SOFA-Framework plugin store) extends functionality significantly.
MoveIt!: MoveIt! is the de facto standard robotics manipulation framework in ROS. Supports robot arms from virtually every major manufacturer (UR, Fanuc, KUKA, ABB, Franka). URDF/SRDF based robot description. Integrates with Gazebo, RViz, and real hardware via ros_control. Annual World MoveIt Day community event. MoveIt Pro (commercial) builds on open-source MoveIt 2.
GraspIt!: GraspIt! was the dominant open-source grasp planning simulator from ~2000-2016. Version 4.0 (April 2021) brought major modernization: switched from qmake to CMake, from custom linear algebra to Eigen3, and added Bullet physics integration. A ROS package (graspit_interface) enables integration with ROS-based manipulation systems. Activity has declined since 2022, with minimal maintenance. The Columbia Grasp Database (CGDB) integration remains a unique feature.
Habitat: Habitat-Sim is designed specifically for embodied AI research at scale — enabling massive RL training with thousands of parallel simulation instances. Supports articulated object simulation (v0.2+), rearrangement tasks (v0.3), and multi-agent scenarios. The Habitat-Matterport 3D Dataset (HM3D) released alongside provides 1000 photorealistic indoor environments. Not primarily a robot dynamics simulator but widely used in embodied agent research. Companion to Habitat-Lab (reinforcement learning training framework).
Project Chrono: Project Chrono is uniquely comprehensive: rigid multibody + FEA + vehicle dynamics + GPU granular mechanics + fluid-structure interaction in one library. Chrono::Vehicle module is particularly advanced for autonomous ground vehicle simulation. PyChrono Python bindings enable scripting. Integration with Blender (Chrono::Blender) for visualization. Used by automotive industry, US Army, and NASA for off-road vehicle simulation.
Flightmare: Flightmare was designed specifically for the drone RL research community. Its unique contribution is the two-mode design: a fast C++ physics model for high-throughput RL training, and an optional Unity renderer for photorealistic data capture. The 1.3k stars reflect impact in the drone RL community. The Scaramuzza lab has since moved to newer drone simulation tools (however Flightmare papers continue to be cited). Dependency on Unity3D for the renderer is a complexity factor.
MRPT: MRPT is one of the most comprehensive single-library robotics toolkits: 100+ SLAM algorithms, 3D visualization, sensor simulation, and mobile robot-specific utilities. Includes mrpt-slam, mrpt-nav, mrpt-vision, mrpt-maps modules. Python bindings (pymrpt) available via pip install pymrpt. ROS 2 integration (mrpt_ros2 packages). Active development since 2005 with one primary maintainer (Blanco-Claraco) is remarkable.
Newton Dynamics: Newton Dynamics is notable for its deterministic exact arithmetic solver, which distinguishes it from other game physics engines like Bullet and PhysX. The v4.x rewrite modernized the codebase significantly (C++17, new parallel solver). The original MADEAPPS/newton-dynamics GitHub repository (~1k stars) has been deprecated in favor of JulioJerez/newton-dynamics. Integrated in Gazebo as an optional physics engine. Used in commercial games and robotics simulations. The zlib license is very permissive (even more permissive than MIT for commercial use).
YARP: YARP is the primary middleware for the iCub humanoid robot — one of the world's most widely used open humanoid platforms (worldwide network of 40+ iCub labs). YARP's port-based communication model provides device abstraction enabling transparency between simulated (Gazebo) and real iCub. YARP has been continuously developed and maintained by IIT since 2006. The robotology superbuild (https://github.com/robotology/robotology-superbuild) provides a comprehensive installation framework for YARP and all IIT robotics software.
OpenRTM-aist: OpenRTM-aist is the de facto standard robotics middleware in Japan, implementing the OMG RT-Component standard (OMG formal/08-01-07). A separate Python implementation (OpenRTM-aist-Python) provides full Python RT-Component framework. There is also OpenRTM-aist-Java. The OMG standardization means OpenRTM components are interoperable with other RT-middleware implementations. Used in Japanese humanoid robots (HRP series), industrial robots, and research platforms. Eclipse-based development tools (RTCBuilder, RTSystemEditor) support GUI-based component development.

Gazebo: or any other thoughts you have),
Webots: or any other thoughts you have), Desktop package installation is straightforward on Linux; validation via bundled demo worlds is simple.
Bullet / PyBullet: Extremely simple pip-based installation. Prebuilt binaries available for Linux, so no compilation needed. The entire installation completes in under a minute. No system-level dependencies beyond Python.
CoppeliaSim (V-REP): Installation is straightforward on Linux — download and extract. No compilation needed. OpenGL library dependencies required for display. CoppeliaSim Edu requires email registration on website before download, adding an extra step compared to pip-based tools.
MuJoCo: Extremely simple installation via pip. Single command installs everything including prebuilt binaries. No compilation required on Linux. Headless rendering supported out of the box via EGL/OSMesa, which is important for server-side RL training.
ODE (Open Dynamics Engine): Installation requires building from source on Linux, which is more involved than pip-based alternatives. The build process follows standard autoconf conventions familiar to Linux developers. Several system-level GL/GLUT dependencies must be installed manually before compilation. Installation completes successfully but requires more steps than modern simulators.
ROS 2 (Robot Operating System 2): Installation via apt on Ubuntu is straightforward and well-documented. The official documentation at docs.ros.org provides clear, step-by-step instructions. The only non-trivial step is manually adding the ROS 2 apt repository. Requires specific Ubuntu version matching the distribution (e.g., Ubuntu 24.04 for Jazzy).
OpenRAVE: Installation is complex and time-consuming. Requires building from source with many system dependencies. The documentation is fragmented across the official website and community wikis. Compatibility with newer Ubuntu versions (22.04+) requires additional patches. Docker-based installation is a simpler alternative but requires Docker knowledge. This is one of the more difficult installations among the simulators reviewed.
PhysX (open-source SDK): Installation via CMake is relatively smooth. The generate_projects.sh script automates much of the configuration. GPU simulation requires an NVIDIA GPU and CUDA Toolkit, but CPU-only mode works on any machine. Documentation is clear and professional.
AirSim: Installation is complex due to the Unreal Engine dependency. Setting up Unreal Engine on Linux from source is a multi-hour process. Using prebuilt environment binaries significantly reduces effort. The airsim Python client installs easily via pip. The Unreal Engine requirement is a significant barrier to entry.
Drake: Exceptionally smooth pip-based installation. Monthly releases published to PyPI make it easy to keep up to date. The apt package option also available for system integration. Documentation clearly specifies supported Python and OS versions. One of the best installation experiences among the tools reviewed.
DART: Clean pip-based installation for Python users via dartpy. System package via apt also straightforward. Building from source uses standard CMake workflow. Installation documentation is clear and up to date. Comparable ease to MuJoCo and Drake pip installs.
CARLA: Installation using prebuilt binaries is simpler than building from source. Requires a discrete GPU (NVIDIA recommended). The Python client installs easily via pip. Building from source requires Unreal Engine 4/5, which is a significant time investment. Headless server mode requires additional Vulkan configuration on Linux.
MORSE: Installation is challenging due to strict Blender 2.79b dependency (discontinued). Modern Ubuntu repositories provide a newer Blender version that is incompatible. Requires manually downloading and installing the legacy Blender 2.79b binary. This is the most significant barrier. The build process itself is straightforward once Blender 2.79b is in place.
Simbody: Conda install is the simplest path (single command). CMake from source is straightforward for a low-level C++ library. Documentation is clear and well-maintained. Primary audience is biomechanics researchers who may prefer OpenSim's GUI over direct Simbody access.
OMPL (Open Motion Planning Library): Extremely easy installation on Ubuntu via apt. Among the simplest C++ library installs reviewed. The apt package includes both the library and Python bindings. Prebuilt packages available on Ubuntu, macOS Homebrew, and via vcpkg on Windows.
Pinocchio: pip install pin is a 2-step install on Linux — one of the simplest C++ physics library installs possible. Conda install provides cross-platform support. Multiple installation paths (pip, conda, apt/robotpkg, source) are all documented clearly.
OROCOS: KDL installation via apt is simple. RTT source build is more complex. Documentation is fragmented across orocos.org and multiple GitHub repositories. Most users install KDL via ROS packages.
SOFA: Prebuilt binary makes installation straightforward. Building SofaPython3 plugin from source requires additional steps. SOFA's GUI and scene-graph paradigm has a learning curve distinct from other simulation tools reviewed.
MoveIt!: Installation is simple once ROS 2 is set up (single apt command). ROS 2 prerequisite installation itself involves several steps (adding apt repo, keys, etc.) but is well-documented. MoveIt 2 documentation is excellent with step-by-step Docker option for a simpler alternative.
GraspIt!: Installation is more complex than average due to older Coin3D/SoQt GUI dependencies. These OpenInventor-based libraries are not commonly installed and can be hard to find on newer Ubuntu versions. Documentation is available but installation reflects the age of the codebase.
Habitat: Conda install is very simple (4 steps). Docker option further simplifies GPU environment setup. Excellent documentation including Docker-based quickstart, Colab notebooks, and conda install instructions. Some 3D datasets require separate download (multi-GB).
Project Chrono: PyChrono conda install is simple (1 command). Full source build with all modules (Vehicle, Granular, FSI, GPU) is complex due to many optional dependencies. Documentation clearly separates installation by module.
Flightmare: Installation of the fast C++ simulation is manageable. The Unity3D renderer requires separate download of prebuilt binaries or building from Unity source. Documentation focused on Ubuntu 18.04/20.04; newer Ubuntu versions may have dependency compatibility issues.
MRPT: Ubuntu PPA install is very simple (3 steps). Personal PPA maintained by Blanco-Claraco ensures always-up-to-date packages. pip install pymrpt is even simpler for Python users. Best documentation of any single-maintainer robotics library reviewed.
Newton Dynamics: Standard CMake build for a physics library. No prebuilt binaries for Linux (Windows has prebuilt DLLs). Documentation adequate but less polished than commercially-backed projects. 6-step source build is typical for C++ physics libraries.
YARP: Conda install is simple (4 steps). robotology-superbuild provides comprehensive one-stop installation for YARP + iCub + Gazebo integration. Documentation covers both simple conda path and full source build with comprehensive options.
OpenRTM-aist: pkg-setup.sh auto-install script is very convenient (1 command). The CORBA dependency (omniORB) is the primary complexity factor compared to simpler middleware. Japanese-language documentation is comprehensive; English documentation is also provided but less complete. pip install openrtm-aist-python (3 commands) for Python users is simpler.

AirSim: The hello_drone.py and hello_car.py examples worked correctly with the prebuilt environments. Photorealistic rendering quality matched documentation screenshots. No CI updates since archival in 2022.
Drake: Highest correctness standards of all reviewed simulators. Tutorial Jupyter notebooks ran flawlessly. The project targets >95% code coverage. Formal verification tools (mathematical proofs via Lyapunov analysis) are a core Drake feature. Russ Tedrake's online textbook uses Drake tutorials as course material.
DART: Tutorial examples ran correctly. CI testing across three platforms provides good correctness assurance. JOSS publication with peer review adds confidence in correctness claims.
CARLA: Getting started examples ran smoothly and matched documentation screenshots. Photorealistic rendering quality is a key correctness feature for sensor simulation fidelity. ScenarioRunner tool enables systematic validation of AV behavior.
MORSE: Tutorial ran correctly with Blender 2.79b. The strict Blender version requirement means tutorials will fail entirely with newer Blender versions. CI is no longer active.
Simbody: Correctness is critically important for biomedical applications. Energy conservation and constraint satisfaction tests in the suite verify physics accuracy. Academic paper and NIH provenance provide strong correctness assurance.
OMPL (Open Motion Planning Library): Built-in benchmarking infrastructure is unique among reviewed tools — allows direct correctness and performance comparison of planning algorithms. Peer-reviewed publication and academic provenance provide strong confidence.
Pinocchio: Analytical derivative verification against numerical finite differences in the test suite is a particularly strong correctness assurance measure. Published benchmark comparisons in IROS 2019 validate computational correctness.
OROCOS: KDL FK/IK correctness well-verified through massive deployment in ROS robots. RTT real-time correctness more complex to formally verify.
SOFA: Medical simulation accuracy requirements drive strong correctness standards. FEM solver validation against analytical solutions and clinical measurements documented in academic papers.
MoveIt!: Excellent CI coverage across multiple ROS 2 distributions. Deployed on real robots globally demonstrates real-world correctness validation. ROS build farm provides system-level integration testing.
GraspIt!: Correctness of grasp quality metrics is validated through the 2004 paper and academic use. Absence of automated tests and CI is a significant weakness for an older codebase. Research papers using GraspIt! serve as de facto correctness validation.
Habitat: Rendering correctness validated through visual benchmarks and comparison with real images. Physics correctness less critical (RL training task) compared to precision simulators. Strong FAIR engineering practices.
Project Chrono: Multi-physics correctness validated through multiple academic publications per module. Vehicle dynamics validation against experimental data documented by UW-Madison SBEL. NSF funding requires reproducibility.
Flightmare: Physics accuracy for RL training validated through successful drone racing policy transfer to real hardware (sim-to-real) in RPG lab publications. Absence of formal automated tests reflects academic prototype nature.
MRPT: Exceptional test coverage for a single-maintainer project (300+ unit tests). Each algorithm class cites original paper. Blanco-Claraco's rigorous approach to SLAM algorithmic correctness is evident throughout the codebase.
Newton Dynamics: Physics correctness implicitly validated through use in commercial games and robotics. Deterministic exact arithmetic solver is the primary correctness guarantee. Absence of CI is a weakness for tracking regressions.
YARP: Correctness validated through real iCub humanoid robot operation in 40+ labs worldwide — strongest possible real-world validation. CI and automated tests supplement operational validation.
OpenRTM-aist: OMG standard compliance provides formal correctness specification. Conformance tested against OMG RT-Component standard test cases. AIST production robot use in HRP humanoid robots validates correctness in real systems.

AirSim: Installation and initial simulation ran reliably. Unreal Engine provides a stable rendering foundation. The archived status means bugs discovered after November 2022 will not receive official fixes.
Drake: Installation and all tutorial notebooks completed without any issues. Monthly release cycle with rigorous CI testing means each release is highly stable. No reliability issues encountered during evaluation.
DART: Stable installation and runtime. No crashes or unexpected shutdowns during tutorial testing. Actively maintained with CI ensuring stability across platforms.
CARLA: Server-client architecture requires both server and client to be running; connection failure if server not started first produces clear error messages. Once running, simulation was stable.
MORSE: The Blender version conflict caused installation failure on first attempt with a clear error message. Once the correct Blender version was installed, the software ran stably. The dependency on an end-of-life software version is a fundamental reliability concern.
Simbody: Simbody is remarkably stable given its role as the physics engine for OpenSim, which is used in clinical settings. Extensive testing driven by biomedical correctness requirements.
OMPL (Open Motion Planning Library): Extremely stable library. Planners are probabilistically complete by design and documented as such. No runtime crashes during tutorial testing.
Pinocchio: Extremely stable. Used in production robotics control systems (e.g., ANYmal quadruped) ensures high reliability requirements are met.
OROCOS: KDL is highly reliable given its use as the core kinematics library in thousands of ROS robots. RTT requires correct POSIX RT kernel configuration for stability.
SOFA: Stable framework with consistent release cadence. A few plugins showed version compatibility issues but core SOFA binary was stable.
MoveIt!: Highly reliable given deployment on physical robot hardware in industrial and research settings. Stability requirements for industrial manipulation applications drive careful release management.
GraspIt!: Core grasp planning functionality is stable for its intended use cases. GUI can exhibit instability with complex scenes. No CI means regression issues may go undetected.
Habitat: Reliable for embodied AI research workloads. GPU memory management for large-scale multi-process training is well-handled. Tutorial notebooks run cleanly.
Project Chrono: Core multibody simulation is very reliable. Optional GPU modules (Chrono::Granular) require correct CUDA setup for stability. Well-tested core dynamics code.
Flightmare: Headless simulation mode is stable for RL training workhorse role. Unity renderer connectivity via ZeroMQ can be fragile across OS versions. Reduced maintenance makes reliability of new OS versions uncertain.
MRPT: 19+ years of continuous development produces a very stable library. The comprehensive CI and test suite catch regressions. Blanco-Claraco's personal investment ensures high quality maintenance.
Newton Dynamics: 20+ years of commercial game use provides industry-level reliability validation. Core rigid body simulation is very stable. v4.x rewrite introduced some rough edges that are being smoothed in ongoing development.
YARP: Exceptional reliability demonstrated through continuous operation on real iCub robots globally. YARP server failure recovery is documented and graceful.
OpenRTM-aist: CORBA-based middleware has proven reliability for distributed systems. AIST institutional maintenance ensures high-quality releases. CORBA naming service dependency requires specific startup sequence.

Webots: or any other thoughts you have), Error reporting is generally clear for invalid world files and missing assets.
Bullet / PyBullet: Generally robust error handling. Descriptive error messages help diagnose issues with robot model files. Some low-level physics parameter errors silently default to fallback values rather than raising exceptions.
CoppeliaSim (V-REP): Error dialogs are informative and guide the user toward resolution. Some edge case errors in Lua scripting produce generic messages rather than specific diagnostics.
MuJoCo: Robust error handling via Python exceptions with clear messages. Invalid MJCF model files generate helpful parse errors indicating line and element. Handles missing textures and meshes gracefully with warnings.
ODE (Open Dynamics Engine): ODE uses a C-style API with return codes rather than exceptions. Error handling is consistent with C library conventions. Invalid inputs generally result in assertion failures or logged error messages.
ROS 2 (Robot Operating System 2): Error messages from the ros2 CLI are descriptive and actionable. Type checking at the middleware level (IDL-defined message types) prevents runtime type mismatches. Launch files with missing nodes raise clear errors.
OpenRAVE: Error messages are generally informative, though some low-level C++ errors surface through Python with less context. COLLADA model loading errors include file path and parse location information.
PhysX (open-source SDK): The PxErrorCallback mechanism provides structured error reporting. Invalid simulation parameters produce descriptive error messages. Memory allocation failures are handled gracefully.
AirSim: Python API error messages are informative. Connection timeout and server unavailability produce clear error messages. JSON configuration file parsing is tolerant of minor formatting variations.
Drake: Excellent error handling with informative exception messages. Optimization infeasibility is caught and reported with solver status details. Type annotations throughout the Python API assist users in providing correct input types.
DART: Error messages from the Python API are descriptive and help identify configuration issues. URDF/SDF parser provides line-number references in error messages.
CARLA: Error handling in the Python client is robust. Connection errors include retry guidance. World and actor API errors are descriptive. Configuration file errors produce line-specific error messages.
MORSE: Component-based Python architecture provides reasonable error handling. Middleware connection failures produce helpful diagnostic messages. The abandoned state means newly discovered robustness issues will not be fixed.
Simbody: Robust error handling appropriate for biomedical computing. Constraint violation errors include state information for debugging. Config errors in model files produce clear diagnostics.
OMPL (Open Motion Planning Library): Library provides clear error messages for misconfigured state spaces and goal regions. Planner timeout behavior is well-defined and configurable.
Pinocchio: Python API error messages are descriptive and include expected vs actual type information. URDF parser errors include element name and attribute context.
OROCOS: KDL uses return-code error handling (rather than exceptions) consistent with real-time embedded design requirements. IK solvers return error codes for unreachable configurations.
SOFA: Scene file error messages include context to help debugging. GPU memory errors for large FEM models produce descriptive CUDA messages.
MoveIt!: Error messages from both C++ and Python APIs are descriptive and actionable. Planning failure messages include reason (timeout, no valid configuration, goal unreachable) and suggested fixes.
GraspIt!: GUI-based interaction limits robustness testing. Some edge cases in grasp quality computation with degenerate configurations produce numerical warnings rather than clean error messages.
Habitat: Good error handling especially for dataset path and GPU configuration issues which are the most common failure modes for new users.
Project Chrono: Good error handling in both C++ and Python APIs. GPU out-of-memory errors for very large granular simulations are descriptive.
Flightmare: Robustness adequate for research use. ZeroMQ socket communication between Python and Unity renderer produces timeout errors on connection failure with limited recovery guidance.
MRPT: Very robust error handling throughout. SLAM algorithms handle degenerate observations gracefully with documented behavior in configuration files.
Newton Dynamics: Error handling functional but less comprehensive than professionally-maintained scientific libraries. Core physics solver robustness is excellent given decades of game industry validation.
YARP: Production robotics middleware requirements drive high robustness standards. Network failure handling is documented and tested. YARP port system is inherently fault-tolerant by design.
OpenRTM-aist: CORBA-based IPC is inherently robust for distributed systems. Component lifecycle management (initiate, activate, deactivate, finalize) handles state transitions safely. Production robot use validates robustness.

Bullet / PyBullet: The PyBullet Quickstart Guide is a comprehensive single-document reference. The community forum and GitHub Issues are active. Assumes basic familiarity with Python and physics simulation concepts.
CoppeliaSim (V-REP): Comprehensive documentation and active forum. The GUI has a learning curve for new users due to its rich feature set. Tutorial series progressively introduces concepts well. Lua scripting knowledge required for advanced usage.
MuJoCo: Excellent documentation and tutorial coverage. GitHub Discussions community is active and responsive. The Jupyter Notebook tutorials are particularly effective for new users. Assumes familiarity with Python and basic physics concepts.
ODE (Open Dynamics Engine): User manual and API documentation are available but less polished than newer simulators. The mailing list is the primary support channel. Community activity has decreased compared to newer physics engines. Requires C/C++ programming knowledge.
ROS 2 (Robot Operating System 2): Exceptional documentation quality at docs.ros.org. Beginner, intermediate, and advanced tutorial tracks. Active community on Discourse and ROS Answers. The learning curve exists (ROS concepts like nodes, topics, services, actions require study) but the documentation handles this well.
OpenRAVE: Documentation was comprehensive at peak activity but has not kept pace with code changes. Community activity has decreased significantly. The mailing list and GitHub Issues are less active than in the software's peak years. Primarily targets experienced robotics researchers.
PhysX (open-source SDK): High-quality documentation and snippet-based getting-started examples. The API is C++ centric and assumes familiarity with physics engine concepts. NVIDIA Developer Forums are active. Primary audience is game developers and robotics engineers.
AirSim: Documentation was comprehensive at time of archival. The GitHub Issues are now read-only. Community support has migrated to Discord and the Colosseum fork. The Unreal Engine requirement adds complexity for users without game development background.
Drake: Outstanding documentation and tutorial ecosystem. The Underactuated Robotics textbook (available free online) provides comprehensive theoretical context. Jupyter Notebook tutorials are interactive and educational. Active Slack community provides fast responses. Requires background in control theory and optimization for effective use.
DART: Documentation is comprehensive for a research-oriented library. The Discourse forum was created in 2019 and provides active community support. Primarily targets robotics researchers and graduate students familiar with multibody dynamics.
CARLA: Excellent documentation quality at carla.readthedocs.io. Step-by-step tutorials for common autonomous driving scenarios. Active Discord community. The GPU requirement and server-client architecture add some complexity for first-time users.
MORSE: Documentation was comprehensive during the project's active life but has not been updated since ~2020. Community support channels are inactive. Unique strength was middleware-agnostic design supporting multiple robotics frameworks simultaneously.
Simbody: Well-documented for its academic audience. Most users access Simbody indirectly through OpenSim rather than directly. The direct C++ API is well-understood by biomechanics and robotics researchers but requires domain expertise.
OMPL (Open Motion Planning Library): Very well-documented for an academic library. Website contains extensive tutorials for C++ and Python users. Benchmarking documentation is particularly thorough. Primarily targets robotics researchers and graduate students.
Pinocchio: Good documentation with Jupyter Notebook tutorials. GitHub Discussions provide an active community support channel. Primarily targets robotics researchers and trajectory optimization practitioners.
OROCOS: Documentation is adequate but fragmented across orocos.org and multiple GitHub repos. KDL documentation better maintained than RTT. Most modern users access KDL through ROS.
SOFA: Good documentation for an academic simulation framework. The scene-graph and component model requires different mental model from code-driven libraries. Annual SOFA Week event builds community engagement.
MoveIt!: Exceptional documentation with Docker quickstart, Jupyter notebooks, and step-by-step tutorials. Large active community via ROS Discourse. MoveIt Setup Assistant GUI simplifies robot configuration. Annual World MoveIt Day brings concentrated community contribution.
GraspIt!: User manual is comprehensive for its era. GUI-based workflow is accessible for researchers but integration with modern ROS/Python workflows requires additional ROS interface package. Reduced maintenance activity since 2022 limits community support.
Habitat: Exceptional interactive documentation: Jupyter notebooks + Colab links for every major tutorial. Habitat Challenge (EmbodiedAI competition) drives community engagement. Discord server provides active support from FAIR team.
Project Chrono: Well-documented with API docs, tutorials, and demo programs for each module. The breadth of modules can be overwhelming for new users. Discord community is active for support.
Flightmare: Well-documented for its intended audience (drone RL researchers). The two-mode architecture (fast vs. photorealistic) is clearly explained. Limited community support as maintenance has declined.
MRPT: Extensive documentation given single-maintainer development. mrpt.org tutorials cover each module. Some tutorials are older and reference GUI tools that may behave slightly differently on new systems.
Newton Dynamics: Documentation has declined from v3.x era. newtondynamics.com forum had active community; GitHub Discussions are less active. API documentation adequate but tutorial content sparse for v4.x series.
YARP: Good documentation for core YARP communication concepts. iCub-specific documentation extensive. New users to distributed robotics middleware may have steeper learning curve for port-based communication concepts.
OpenRTM-aist: Comprehensive Japanese documentation available at openrtm.org; English documentation adequate but less complete than Japanese. Eclipse-based RTCBuilder GUI tool aids component development. CORBA concepts can be steep learning curve for new users outside Japan.

Core library has 3 GitHub contributors; broader contributor base through organization. Commercial model means most development happens internally.
MuJoCo: Actively maintained by Google DeepMind. 44 GitHub releases since open-sourcing in 2022. 99 contributors. Rapid release cadence (multiple releases per year). Contributor guide is clear and welcoming.
ODE (Open Dynamics Engine): Longest-active project among the simulators reviewed. Version 0.16.6 shows the project is still maintained. Development pace is slower than newer projects. Uses Bitbucket rather than GitHub, which reduces community visibility.
ROS 2 (Robot Operating System 2): Exemplary maintainability. Annual releases with clear LTS policy. REP process provides formal governance for significant changes. Multiple active maintainers per repository. Rapid issue response time. vcstool and rosdep tools automate workspace management.
OpenRAVE: Reduced maintenance activity in recent years. 10,375 total commits reflect a long development history but activity has declined. No formal contribution guide. Many open issues remain unaddressed. The Python 3 fork (openrave3) represents community-driven maintenance.
PhysX (open-source SDK): Professionally maintained by NVIDIA. Regular releases tied to NVIDIA Omniverse updates. Migration guide provided for version transitions. Both PhysX 4.x and 5.x maintained in separate GitHub repositories.
AirSim: Repository archived by Microsoft in November 2022 with no future updates planned. The Colosseum fork (CodexLabsLLC/Colosseum) is the de facto community continuation. Maintenance score reflects the archived status.
Drake: Best-in-class software engineering practices for a research codebase. Monthly releases, comprehensive CI, enforced style guide, detailed contributor documentation, and high issue closure rate. Toyota Research Institute investment ensures long-term maintenance.
DART: Active maintenance with dual-branch strategy (stable release-6.x and forward-looking main). Multi-institutional maintenance reduces risk of abandonment. Monthly PyPI releases of dartpy ensure accessible Python updates.
CARLA: Active maintenance with major version releases. Dual branch strategy (ue5-dev and ue4-dev). CVPR challenge drives annual development momentum. The 0.10.0 UE5 migration was a significant engineering effort. CARLA-ROS-Bridge and ScenarioRunner maintained as separate repositories.
MORSE: Project is unmaintained since ~2020. No new releases since MORSE 1.4 (2016). The fundamental dependency on Blender 2.79b (itself end-of-life in 2018) means MORSE cannot easily be updated. Low maintainability score reflects current state rather than historical quality.
Simbody: Maintained by a small but dedicated team at Stanford with NIH support continuity. Simbody's central role in OpenSim ensures continued maintenance is essential. Slow release cadence but high stability.
OMPL (Open Motion Planning Library): Steadily maintained by Kavraki Lab with regular releases. The 1.7.0 release included contributions from 19 researchers. Long-term maintenance supported by NSF and the academic need for a standard motion planning benchmarking platform.
Pinocchio: Very actively maintained by Inria with frequent releases. The jump to Pinocchio 3.x (2024) demonstrated willingness for significant API evolution. Strong institutional backing ensures long-term maintenance.
OROCOS: KDL actively maintained through its role in ROS; RTT is less active. Multi-repository structure makes maintenance tracking complex. KDL's role as a ROS dependency ensures critical fixes are addressed.
SOFA: Active development with biannual version releases (YY.06 and YY.12). Four Inria teams provide institutional maintenance continuity. Plugin ecosystem maintained separately by community.
MoveIt!: Excellent maintenance by PickNik Robotics with professional software engineering practices. PickNik's commercial MoveIt Pro product ensures sustained investment in open-source MoveIt. Google Summer of Code brings fresh contributors annually.
GraspIt!: Declining maintenance is the most significant concern. The v4.0 modernization was a positive step but subsequent activity dropped significantly. The tool remains functional for its original purpose but has not been actively adapted to modern software ecosystems.
Habitat: Strongly maintained by FAIR with dedicated team. Rapid iteration tied to Habitat Challenge competition cadence. Facebook CLA for contributions is standard for Meta open-source projects.
Project Chrono: Steadily maintained with major version releases roughly annually. NSF and US Army funding continuity ensures long-term maintenance. UW-Madison SBEL lab has graduate student continuity for development.
Flightmare: Research code artifact rather than actively maintained software product. RPG lab has shifted to newer drone simulation platforms. Open issues remain unresolved. Academic prototype with high citation impact but limited ongoing support.
MRPT: Remarkable for a primary single-maintainer project. Blanco-Claraco maintains professional software engineering standards (CI, tests, CHANGELOG, versioning). 19+ years of continuous maintenance demonstrates exceptional commitment.
Newton Dynamics: Single primary maintainer creates sustainability risk. The v4.x rewrite was substantial but created migration burden for existing users. Long history (20+ years) demonstrates commitment but also shows challenges of personal-project maintenance at scale.
YARP: IIT institutional commitment ensures long-term maintenance tied to iCub robot program. robology-superbuild provides coordinated release of YARP with other IIT tools. Regular versioned releases with maintained changelog.
OpenRTM-aist: AIST government laboratory institutional maintenance provides reliable long-term support. November 2024 release confirms active maintenance. Dual LGPL/EPL+commercial license model provides sustainable funding path. METI government support continues.

Gazebo: or any other thoughts you have),
Webots: or any other thoughts you have), Supports multiple programming languages (C, C++, Python, Java, MATLAB). Provides APIs for robot control and sensor access. Pre-built robot models and environments available.
Bullet / PyBullet: Python bindings to Bullet Physics C++ SDK. Supports integration with TensorFlow, OpenAI Gym, and other ML frameworks. Widely used for creating custom RL environments.
CoppeliaSim (V-REP): Highly modular plugin-based architecture. Remote API supports Python, Lua, Java, MATLAB, Octave, C, C++, Rust. Supports multiple physics engines (MuJoCo, Bullet, ODE, Newton, Vortex). Integrates with ROS and ROS2.
MuJoCo: C API and Python bindings (mujoco-py). Used as backend by many RL frameworks (OpenAI Gym, dm_control, Gymnasium). Supports MJCF (MuJoCo XML) and URDF model formats. Bindings available for C++, Python, C#/Unity.
ODE (Open Dynamics Engine): C/C++ library API is stable and well-documented. Widely integrated into other simulators (Gazebo Classic, CoppeliaSim, Webots). Bindings available for Python, Java, and other languages via third-party projects. The stable API has allowed it to remain a standard backend for decades.
ROS 2 (Robot Operating System 2): Highly reusable modular architecture based on packages. Client library interfaces (rcl) available for C++ (rclcpp) and Python (rclpy). Supports composition to combine multiple nodes into a single process. Message and service interfaces defined in IDL and auto-generated for multiple languages. Compatible with ROS 1 via ros1_bridge.
OpenRAVE: Highly modular plugin architecture: planners, controllers, sensors, and IK solvers are all plugins. IKFast is a notable sub-tool for generating fast analytical IK solutions. Python API allows scripting and integration with other systems. Supports COLLADA and URDF robot models.
PhysX (open-source SDK): Stable, well-documented C++ API used by major game engines (Unreal Engine, Unity) and robotics simulators (Isaac Sim, CoppeliaSim). Python bindings available via omni.physx in NVIDIA Omniverse. Supports GPU and CPU simulation paths for flexibility.
AirSim: Multi-language API support (Python, C++, ROS, MATLAB, Java). AirLib (the core physics library) is designed to be independent of Unreal Engine for portability. Python API provides simple and consistent interface for sensor data and vehicle control.
Drake: Highly modular architecture with clear separation between multibody dynamics (MultibodyPlant), systems (LeafSystem/DiagramBuilder), optimization (MathematicalProgram), and perception. Python bindings (pydrake) mirror the C++ API. Integrates with MuJoCo for contact simulation. Supports URDF and SDFormat robot models.
DART: Modular architecture separating dynamics, collision detection, optimizer, and GUI components. Python bindings (dartpy) mirror C++ API. Supports URDF, SDF, and VSK skeleton formats. Used as physics backend in Gazebo, and in OpenAI/Gymnasium environments.
CARLA: Python API is well-designed for scripting scenarios. Supports ROS/ROS 2 integration via CARLA-ROS-Bridge. OpenDRIVE standard map format ensures compatibility with traffic scenario tools. Used as environment in major AV research benchmarks (CARLA Leaderboard 2.0).
MORSE: Unique middleware-agnostic architecture allows connecting to ROS, YARP, and Pocolibs simultaneously — a design feature not found in most simulators. Component-based architecture (sensors, actuators, robots as composable objects) is highly reusable. However, the Blender 2.79b dependency severely limits current practical reusability.
Simbody: Clean C++ API with CMake integration. Used in OpenSim (clinical biomechanics), various robotics frameworks, and vehicle dynamics simulation. Apache-2.0 license supports commercial use.
OMPL (Open Motion Planning Library): Extremely reusable by design — OMPL is explicitly designed as a library to be integrated into other frameworks. Used in MoveIt, OpenRAVE, Gazebo, and numerous custom robotics systems. Plugin architecture for custom planners and state spaces.
Pinocchio: Highly reusable: extensively header-only design, clean Python bindings via pip install pin, URDF/SRDF model format support, CMake integration. Used in ANYmal, Atlas, and other major robot control frameworks. crocoddyl (trajectory optimizer) built directly on Pinocchio.
OROCOS: KDL highly reusable — forms basis of most ROS kinematics tools. LGPL license enables commercial use. RTT component model enables reusable real-time control blocks.
SOFA: Component-based architecture is highly reusable. Plugin system enables extension without modifying core. SofaPython3 enables scripting and integration with machine learning frameworks. LGPL core allows commercial use with open plugin sharing.
MoveIt!: Highly reusable plugin architecture: planning algorithms, kinematics solvers, collision checkers all swappable as plugins. Python MoveIt interface wraps C++ API. Supports virtually all major robot arms via URDF. Used in manufacturing, surgical robotics, and research globally.
GraspIt!: Reusable as a standalone grasp simulator for research. ROS interface (graspit_interface) enables integration. GPL-2.0 license restricts commercial use without license compliance. Dependency on Coin3D/SoQt is a modern reusability barrier.
Habitat: Highly reusable Python API. MIT license enables commercial and research use. Conda install simplicity and Docker images lower integration barrier. Used in hundreds of embodied AI papers. Habitat-Lab provides RL training framework built on top of Habitat-Sim.
Project Chrono: BSD-3 license enables commercial use. CMake integration for embedding in other projects. PyChrono enables Python integration. Used by automotive companies, defense (US Army), and space agencies for digital twin simulation.
Flightmare: MIT license and modular design enable reuse. ZeroMQ-based renderer communication is reusable pattern. OpenAI Gym-compatible environment interface enables plug-in with standard RL frameworks. Declining maintenance limits long-term reusability.
MRPT: BSD-3 license enables commercial use. CMake integration (find_package(MRPT)), ROS 2 integration, and pymrpt Python bindings provide multiple integration paths. 30+ MRPT sub-libraries can be individually linked for minimal dependency footprint.
Newton Dynamics: Zlib license is very permissive (even for closed-source commercial use). C API with C++ wrapper enables integration in any language via FFI. Python and Lua bindings available. Widely integrated as Gazebo optional physics engine and in game engines (OGRE3D, Irrlicht). API stability within major version maintained.
YARP: BSD-3 license enables commercial use. SWIG-generated Python and Java bindings. CMake integration (find_package(YARP)). Gazebo integration (gazebo-yarp-plugins) for simulation. Core YARP port concept is portable across all supported OS.
OpenRTM-aist: OMG standard compliance ensures interoperability with other RT-middleware implementations. CORBA IDL interface definitions provide language-neutral component specifications. LGPL license enables commercial use with some restrictions; commercial license removes restrictions. Python implementation (OpenRTM-aist-Python) increases accessibility.

Webots: or any other thoughts you have), Code organization is generally clear, with some legacy modules less documented.
Bullet / PyBullet: Code is reasonably well-structured. The C++ core follows a consistent naming convention. Python wrapper code (examples/*.py) is clear and well-commented. Some older C++ source files have less documentation than newer additions.
CoppeliaSim (V-REP): Code is generally readable and well-organized. No explicit coding standard document, but consistent style throughout. The modular plugin architecture aids understandability. Some internal physics modules have complex, dense code with limited comments.
MuJoCo: Code follows Google C++ style consistently. Well-organized into modules (engine, render, UI, Python). Header files are well-commented with parameter descriptions. Overall high code quality consistent with a Google-maintained project.
ODE (Open Dynamics Engine): Mature, readable C/C++ codebase. Consistent coding style throughout. No formal coding standard document, but style is uniform. Well-organized into modules: ode/src (core), ode/include (headers), ode/demo (examples). Comments are clear and informative.
ROS 2 (Robot Operating System 2): Excellent code quality across randomly sampled source files from rclcpp and rcl. Consistent style enforced by ament_lint. Well-structured layered architecture (rcl → rclcpp → user code). Comprehensive inline documentation enabling Doxygen generation. One of the best-organized open-source robotics codebases reviewed.
OpenRAVE: Code is generally readable and well-organized into modules (planning, kinematics, collision, rendering). Some older C++ files have inconsistent comment density. The plugin architecture aids modularity. A few numerical constants are hard-coded without named definitions.
PhysX (open-source SDK): Professional-grade C++ code with clear naming conventions. Well-organized into subsystems (geometry, scene, cooking, vehicle, character controller). Px prefix used consistently for all public API types. Snippet examples are particularly well-written and instructive.
AirSim: Well-structured code with clear separation between AirLib (core physics/sensor models), the Unreal Engine plugin, and the Python client. Consistent Microsoft Research coding style. Physics vehicle models are clearly commented.
Drake: Highest code quality of all tools reviewed. Google C++ Style Guide strictly enforced. Every class and function has Doxygen documentation. Algorithm citations in comments throughout. Template-heavy C++ is complex but consistently structured. Best-in-class academic+industrial software engineering.
DART: Well-structured academic C++ codebase with clear module separation. Algorithm references in comments aid understanding. Template-heavy dynamics code requires C++ expertise but is logically organized.
CARLA: Well-organized codebase with clear separation between server (C++ Unreal Engine) and client (Python). Sensor actors, vehicle physics, and traffic management are cleanly modularized. Python client code is particularly readable.
MORSE: Well-structured Python codebase with clear component hierarchy (sensors, actuators, robots, environments all as reusable Python classes). Blender API calls are isolated from core simulation logic. Readable for Python developers.
Simbody: Well-structured academic C++ library. Heavy use of templates for numerical types. Physics-domain knowledge required to fully understand dynamics code, but module organization is logical.
OMPL (Open Motion Planning Library): Exemplary academic C++ library design. Each planner is a clearly bounded class. Algorithm papers cited in Doxygen. Consistent interface across all 30+ planners makes the codebase highly readable.
Pinocchio: Well-organized template C++ code. Algorithm references in every dynamics function aid understanding for robotics practitioners. Can be complex for developers unfamiliar with Eigen and Lie group mathematics.
OROCOS: KDL code well-organized around kinematic chain abstraction. RTT more complex due to real-time threading. C++ style is somewhat dated in older files.
SOFA: Well-structured C++ code with clear component hierarchy. Scene graph model is well-abstracted. Complex template-based data types can be challenging for newcomers.
MoveIt!: Well-structured codebase following ROS conventions. Plugin interfaces clearly defined. ROS-based communication model may require ROS knowledge to fully understand but is well-documented within the ROS ecosystem.
GraspIt!: Code reflects its academic origin and age (C++ style circa 2004). Qt GUI code is interleaved with physics simulation code in some modules. Core grasp quality computation code is clear and algorithm-referenced.
Habitat: Well-structured Python and C++ code following FAIR engineering standards. Enum-based configuration system (Habitat Config) is well-documented. Notebook-based documentation aids understanding significantly.
Project Chrono: Well-organized modular structure. Each module (MBS, Vehicle, FEA, etc.) is self-contained. Physics domain knowledge required to fully understand advanced modules. Demo programs effectively illustrate each feature area.
Flightmare: Clean, readable research code. The separation between physics simulator and renderer is architecturally clear. NeurIPS paper provides the best documentation of design decisions.
MRPT: Exemplary academic C++ library with excellent modularization. Every algorithm class includes Doxygen with paper citations. Consistent C++17 code style throughout. Sub-library organization makes navigation of 1000+ files manageable.
Newton Dynamics: Code reflects a long-lived personal project by a single expert author. Physics domain knowledge required for full comprehension. The v4.x rewrite improved code organization significantly over v3.x.
YARP: Well-organized C++ library with consistent IIT coding standards. Port/carrier/device abstraction hierarchy is clearly defined and consistently implemented across 1000+ files.
OpenRTM-aist: Well-organized CORBA middleware following OMG standard structure. Some comments in Japanese are a barrier for non-Japanese users. AIST institutional standards result in professional code quality.

Gazebo: or any other thoughts you have),
Webots: or any other thoughts you have), Transparent open-source development with active community involvement
Bullet / PyBullet: Open development process with active community involvement. Release notes provided for each version. GitHub Issues and pull requests give clear visibility into development activity.
CoppeliaSim (V-REP): Commercial development with partially open-source core. Release history is publicly tracked. Internal development roadmap not publicly disclosed, which is typical for commercial software.
MuJoCo: Fully transparent open-source development under Google DeepMind. Versioning and changelog well-documented. Every release includes detailed notes on new features, bug fixes, and API changes.
ODE (Open Dynamics Engine): Development is visible through Bitbucket but less accessible than GitHub-hosted projects. Changelog and release tags provide adequate version history. Community transparency is lower than newer Google/DeepMind-maintained projects.
ROS 2 (Robot Operating System 2): Fully transparent development. Public roadmap, formal proposal process (REPs), detailed release notes, and open CI infrastructure. Community-driven governance through OSR Alliance since April 2024.
OpenRAVE: Development is visible through GitHub but informal. No defined roadmap or governance model. Changelog exists but is not consistently maintained. Reduced activity makes future development direction unclear.
PhysX (open-source SDK): Transparent development on GitHub with clear versioning. NVIDIA publishes blog posts for major releases explaining new features. Release notes and migration guides are thorough.
AirSim: Development history is fully visible through GitHub. The archival decision was communicated publicly. Future direction shifted to Microsoft Project AirSim as a commercial product.
Drake: Fully transparent open development. Monthly release schedule is published and consistently followed. Detailed CHANGELOG for every release. GitHub Issues and PRs provide complete development visibility.
DART: Transparent development on GitHub with well-documented release history. CI status visible via GitHub Actions badges. JOSS peer review provides formal process transparency.
CARLA: Public roadmap visible through GitHub milestones and CARLA blog. Annual CVPR challenge provides a structured development and evaluation cycle. Release notes are detailed and include migration guides.
MORSE: Development history is fully visible through GitHub. The project's end-of-life status is documented in the README. Historical transparency was good; current transparency is limited to the archived state of the repository.
Simbody: Development history fully visible on GitHub since October 2013 migration. Academic publication record provides transparent design rationale. NIH grant history documents funding transparency.
OMPL (Open Motion Planning Library): Highly transparent development process. Academic origin ensures design decisions are documented in peer-reviewed literature. CI status badges visible in README.
Pinocchio: Transparent development with clear release cadence. Inria institutional web presence provides visibility into team structure. CI badges prominently displayed in README.
OROCOS: Development history visible through GitHub and historical orocos.org. Multi-component structure reduces transparency. KDL development well-documented through ROS ecosystem.
SOFA: Transparent biannual release schedule. Inria team affiliation provides institutional transparency. Roadmap communicated through SOFA Week conference and GitHub milestones.
MoveIt!: Exemplary development transparency. PickNik's public roadmap, World MoveIt Day reports, and ROS Discourse communications provide excellent visibility. CI status and release schedules are publicly posted.
GraspIt!: Development history partially visible through GitHub. Academic papers provide design rationale. Declining activity makes current development status opaque.
Habitat: Development highly transparent through FAIR publications and active GitHub. Annual Habitat Challenge drives public roadmap visibility. aihabitat.org provides clear version history.
Project Chrono: Highly transparent: NSF-funded research requires public documentation. SBEL website details current research activities. CI statistics publicly visible on projectchrono.org.
Flightmare: Academic research transparency through NeurIPS paper. GitHub commit history provides development visibility. Lack of formal release process reduces visibility for users tracking changes.
MRPT: High development transparency despite primarily single-maintainer structure. CI status badges in README. Detailed CHANGELOG documents every release. Personal PPA provides visibility into release cadence.
Newton Dynamics: Development visibility acceptable for personal project. newtondynamics.com blog historically provided good insight into design decisions. GitHub migration consolidated development tracking.
YARP: High transparency from both open technical development (GitHub) and institutional publication channels (EU project deliverables, IIT papers). robotology organization GitHub provides integrated view of all IIT robotics software.
OpenRTM-aist: Development transparency adequate for government laboratory project. OMG standard compliance process provides formal visibility into interface changes. Japanese-language documentation dominates but GitHub communication is in English.
